_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Hinds County, MS. Formed in 1821 from Choctaw cession lands. Jackson, the state capital, is the county seat. Hinds County is unique in Mississippi for having two courthouses — one in Jackson and one in Raymond — a dual-courthouse arrangement that persists to the present. The county's records benefit from Jackson's status as the state capital, with the Mississippi Department of Archives and History located in the same city.

current_courthouse.location: Jackson, MS — 407 East Pascagoula Street (Hinds County Courthouse, First Judicial District). Raymond, MS — courthouse square (Hinds County Courthouse, Second Judicial District, built 1857-1859, one of the finest antebellum courthouses in Mississippi).

previous_courthouses.location: Raymond, MS — Raymond served as the original county seat before Jackson was established as the state capital
previous_courthouses.fate: Records were split when Jackson became the primary county seat. The Raymond courthouse continued to serve the Second Judicial District.

previous_courthouses.location: Jackson — various buildings served as the county courthouse; Raymond continued separately
previous_courthouses.fate: Jackson was burned by Union forces under General William T. Sherman in July 1863 and again in February 1864. The destruction was substantial — Jackson was called 'Chimneyville' afterward. However, some county records had been removed before the burning. The Raymond courthouse, six miles southwest of the fighting, survived intact.

previous_courthouses.location: Jackson — rebuilt courthouse facilities; Raymond courthouse (1857-1859) continued in use
previous_courthouses.fate: Jackson rebuilt after the war. The 1930 courthouse replaced earlier postwar structures.

record_losses.cause: Union forces burned Jackson during the Vicksburg Campaign (July 1863)
records_destroyed: some court records stored in Jackson
records_survived: Raymond district records (not in the path of destruction)
some Jackson records removed before the burning
records that had been duplicated at the state level
record_losses.description: Sherman's forces burned much of Jackson during the Vicksburg Campaign, earning the city the name 'Chimneyville.' County officials had some warning and removed records from the path of destruction, but the chaos of the campaign meant the evacuation was incomplete. The Raymond courthouse — located outside the theater of operations — survived untouched, preserving the Second Judicial District's records intact. The dual-courthouse system, which might seem a bureaucratic oddity, proved to be an inadvertent form of records insurance.
record_losses.narrative_impact: If {ancestor_name}'s records were filed in the Jackson courthouse, some may have been lost when Sherman burned the city. If they were filed in Raymond, they almost certainly survived. The dual-courthouse system means the researcher must check both locations — and the split may have saved records that a single courthouse would have lost entirely.
record_losses.source: Bearss, Edwin C. The Campaign for Vicksburg. 3 vols. Dayton, OH: Morningside Press, 1985-1986.

records_destroyed: additional records that had survived or been returned after the 1863 burning
record_losses.description: Jackson was burned a second time in February 1864 by Union forces under General Sherman during the Meridian Campaign. This second destruction further damaged whatever county records had survived or been returned after the July 1863 burning. The cumulative effect of two burnings was severe for records housed in Jackson.
record_losses.narrative_impact: Jackson was burned twice in seven months. If {ancestor_name}'s court filings were in the Jackson courthouse and survived the first fire, they may not have survived the second. The double destruction makes Raymond's intact records all the more valuable.
record_losses.source: Marszalek, John F. Sherman's March to the Sea. Abilene, TX: McWhiney Foundation Press, 2005.

deeds.completeness: Variable — Raymond district deeds survive from county formation. Jackson district deeds have gaps from the 1863-1864 burnings. Later deed books (post-1870) are generally complete.
deeds.gaps: Jackson district records 1821-1865 have significant gaps due to the two burnings. Raymond district records are more complete.
deeds.repository: Hinds County Chancery Clerk, Jackson (First District) and Raymond (Second District); Mississippi Department of Archives and History
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; MDAH digital collections; Ancestry.com MS land records

wills_estates.completeness: Raymond district records survive better than Jackson district for the antebellum period. Post-war records are generally complete for both districts.
wills_estates.gaps: Jackson district estate records from the antebellum period have gaps. Raymond district is more intact.
wills_estates.repository: Hinds County Chancery Clerk (both districts); Mississippi Department of Archives and History

marriages.completeness: Partial before 1865 due to Jackson burnings. Better from Raymond district. Post-war records are complete.
marriages.gaps: Jackson district pre-1865 marriage records are incomplete.
marriages.repository: Hinds County Circuit Clerk; Mississippi Department of Archives and History

court_minutes.completeness: Raymond district court minutes survive from county formation. Jackson district has gaps from the Civil War period.
court_minutes.gaps: Jackson district court minutes from 1863-1865 are fragmentary.
court_minutes.repository: Hinds County Circuit Clerk (both districts); Mississippi Department of Archives and History

vital_records.completeness: Mississippi statewide registration began 1912. Jackson, as the state capital, had somewhat better early compliance than rural areas.
vital_records.repository: Mississippi State Department of Health, Vital Records

substitute_sources.source_type: Mississippi Department of Archives and History collections
substitute_sources.description: Because Jackson is the state capital, MDAH holds extensive duplicate and complementary records for Hinds County. State-level records — legislative journals, governor's papers, land office records, and Supreme Court case files — often contain copies of county-level documents that were destroyed in the 1863-1864 burnings.
substitute_sources.repository: Mississippi Department of Archives and History, Jackson (located in the same city as the county courthouse)
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: MDAH is the most important substitute source for Hinds County records lost in the war. Supreme Court appeals contain transcripts of lower court proceedings. Legislative petitions name individuals. Governor's correspondence includes pardons and commutations.
substitute_sources.source: Mississippi Department of Archives and History. Guide to Official Records in the Mississippi Department of Archives and History. Jackson, 1994.

substitute_sources.description: The Raymond courthouse survived the Civil War intact. Records for the Second Judicial District — the western, more rural portion of Hinds County — are complete from county formation. For families who lived in the Raymond district, these records fill gaps that the Jackson burnings created.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: If the ancestor lived in the western part of Hinds County, Raymond records may be complete where Jackson records are not. Even for Jackson-district families, Raymond records may contain related transactions or proceedings.
substitute_sources.source: Hinds County court records; MDAH holdings.

substitute_sources.description: Population schedules 1830-1940 (Hinds County was formed in 1821; first enumerated in 1830). Slave schedules 1850-1860.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Federal census records survived in Washington and are essential for bridging the gaps in Jackson-district county records. The 1850 and 1860 censuses are the most detailed pre-war records available for families whose county records burned.
substitute_sources.source: National Archives and Records Administration. Federal Census Schedules, Hinds County, MS.

narrative_hooks: Jackson earned the name 'Chimneyville' because Sherman burned it twice — July 1863 and February 1864. If {ancestor_name}'s records were in the Jackson courthouse, they survived two fires or they did not. The answer may depend on which judicial district the family lived in, because the Raymond courthouse never burned at all.

Hinds County has two courthouses — one in Jackson, one in Raymond — and that administrative peculiarity may have saved {ancestor_name}'s records from the war. Raymond was six miles from the fighting and a century of unbroken record-keeping sits in its antebellum courthouse to this day.

Jackson was the state capital, and the state archives are in the same city as the county courthouse. If {ancestor_name}'s county records burned with 'Chimneyville,' duplicate copies may survive at the Mississippi Department of Archives and History — because the state kept its own records of what the county was doing.

When {ancestor_name} appeared in Hinds County court, the question for the genealogist is which courthouse: Jackson or Raymond. The answer determines whether the Civil War destroyed the record or left it untouched on a courthouse shelf in a town the war barely noticed.
source_references: Mississippi Department of Archives and History. Guide to Official Records in the Mississippi Department of Archives and History. Jackson, 1994.
Bearss, Edwin C. The Campaign for Vicksburg. 3 vols. Dayton, OH: Morningside Press, 1985-1986.
Marszalek, John F. Sherman's March to the Sea. Abilene, TX: McWhiney Foundation Press, 2005.
National Archives and Records Administration. Federal Census Schedules, Hinds County, MS.
